questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Assignment of lease - original tenant and assignee liable for rent
questions.stem: A tenant leased a warehouse for five years under a written lease requiring monthly rent. Two years later, the tenant transferred the entire remaining lease term to a distributor. The landlord gave written consent to the transfer but did not release the tenant from any lease obligation. The distributor took possession and paid rent for six months, then stopped paying while remaining in possession. The lease contains no unusual limitation on assignment.
questions.call: Against whom may the landlord recover the unpaid rent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Against both the original tenant, based on privity of contract, and the distributor, based on privity of estate.
choices.rationale: An assignment does not ordinarily release the original tenant's contractual duties, and the assignee in possession is liable on lease covenants that run with the estate, including rent.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Against the distributor only, because assigning the entire term automatically releases the original tenant.
choices.rationale: Assignment transfers the estate but does not constitute a novation or release of the original tenant's contractual liability.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Against the original tenant only, because an assignee who did not sign the original lease cannot be liable for rent.
choices.rationale: An assignee in privity of estate is liable for covenants that touch and concern the leasehold, including the rent covenant.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Against neither party, because the landlord's consent extinguished the rent covenant.
choices.rationale: Consent to assignment permits the transfer; without an express release or novation, it does not extinguish the rent obligations.

explanation.testedIssue: Who remains liable for rent after a complete lease assignment with consent but no release.
explanation.governingRule: The original tenant remains liable on the lease through privity of contract unless released. An assignee who holds the leasehold estate is in privity of estate with the landlord and is liable for covenants that run with the land, including rent, while the assignee remains in possession.
explanation.application: The transfer covered the entire remaining term, making it an assignment. The landlord consented but did not release the tenant, and the distributor remains the assignee in possession, so both are liable.
explanation.commonTrap: Confusing permission to assign with a novation releasing the original tenant.
explanation.memoryAid: Assignment adds an assignee; it does not erase the original contract.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Easement appurtenant after subdivision of dominant estate
questions.stem: A recorded deed granted the owner of a 40-acre tract an easement over a neighboring parcel 'for access between the public road and the 40-acre tract.' Years later, the owner divided the 40 acres into four residential lots and sold them to separate buyers. Each buyer uses the easement to reach a single home. The combined traffic is somewhat greater than when the tract contained one farmhouse but remains ordinary residential traffic and does not materially interfere with the servient parcel. The servient owner seeks to bar use by three of the four lots.
questions.call: Is the servient owner likely to succeed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because subdivision automatically extinguishes an easement appurtenant as to every parcel except the first parcel sold.
choices.rationale: An appurtenant easement ordinarily passes with each subdivided portion of the dominant estate, subject to the scope and burden limits.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because the subdivided lots may share the easement absent an unreasonable increase in burden.
choices.rationale: The grant benefited the entire 40-acre tract, and ordinary residential use by its subdivisions is permissible absent an unreasonable additional burden.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because any increase in traffic beyond the use existing when the easement was created is impermissible.
choices.rationale: Reasonable evolution and increased use are allowed when consistent with the easement's purpose and not unreasonably burdensome.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because an easement appurtenant may also be used to benefit any later-acquired land adjoining the dominant estate.
choices.rationale: Subdivision of the original dominant estate is generally permitted, but extending the easement to separate after-acquired land is a different and often impermissible use.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an access easement appurtenant benefits subdivided portions of the original dominant estate.
explanation.governingRule: An easement appurtenant generally passes to each portion of a subdivided dominant estate if the use remains within the grant's purpose and does not unreasonably increase the burden on the servient estate. It ordinarily may not be extended to benefit property outside the original dominant estate.
explanation.application: The deed benefited the full 40 acres, and all four lots came from that tract. Their ordinary residential traffic is within the access purpose and is expressly described as not materially interfering.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating subdivision as prohibited or equating it with adding unrelated after-acquired land.
explanation.memoryAid: Subdivided dominant land shares the easement, unless the burden becomes unreasonable.

questions.subject: Torts
questions.subtopic: Respondeat superior - minor personal detour during assigned route
questions.stem: A bakery instructed a delivery driver to take a company van along a fixed route to deliver bread to several restaurants. Between scheduled stops, the driver pulled into a coffee shop located directly on the route, intending to buy coffee and resume deliveries within five minutes. As the driver pulled out of the shop's parking lot to continue toward the next restaurant, he negligently struck a pedestrian. The bakery had a written rule forbidding personal stops while making deliveries.
questions.call: Is the bakery vicariously liable for the pedestrian's injury?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because violating the bakery's personal-stop rule necessarily placed the driver outside the scope of employment.
choices.rationale: An employee may remain within the scope despite violating an employer's instruction; the degree and purpose of the deviation control.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because buying coffee served no purpose of the bakery.
choices.rationale: A brief personal detour can be incidental to employment even though the immediate errand is personal.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because the brief stop was a minor detour and the driver was resuming assigned deliveries.
choices.rationale: A slight deviation for personal comfort ordinarily remains within the scope, particularly when the employee is returning to the assigned route.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the bakery was independently negligent in hiring the driver.
choices.rationale: Vicarious liability does not require proof of the employer's own negligence when the employee's tort occurred within the scope of employment.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a short personal stop during an assigned delivery route is a detour or a frolic.
explanation.governingRule: An employer is vicariously liable for an employee's tort committed within the scope of employment. A minor deviation for personal purposes is generally a detour within the scope, while a substantial abandonment of the employer's business is a frolic outside it. Violation of an instruction is relevant but not conclusive.
explanation.application: The driver remained on the assigned route, planned only a five-minute stop, and was already returning to the delivery mission when the collision occurred. The deviation was slight.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating any personal purpose or rule violation as an automatic frolic.
explanation.memoryAid: Small detour stays in scope; substantial abandonment does not.

questions.subject: Torts
questions.subtopic: Private necessity - privilege to enter but liability for actual property damage
questions.stem: During an unexpected and unusually violent storm, a sailor reasonably believed that his boat would be destroyed unless he tied it to a privately owned pier. The pier owner ordered him to leave, but the sailor remained tied to the pier until the storm passed. The boat survived, but its repeated impact caused $8,000 in damage to the pier. The sailor had not negligently created the emergency.
questions.call: What is the sailor's tort liability to the pier owner?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The sailor is liable for trespass and punitive damages because he remained after the owner ordered him to leave.
choices.rationale: Private necessity privileged the continued entry while the emergency lasted, defeating ordinary trespass liability and punitive damages absent misconduct.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The sailor has no liability because private necessity is a complete privilege that also shifts all resulting loss to the property owner.
choices.rationale: Private necessity is an incomplete privilege: it permits the invasion but generally requires payment for actual damage caused.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The sailor is liable only if a reasonable person would have chosen to sacrifice the boat instead.
choices.rationale: The facts establish a reasonable necessity; liability for actual damage does not depend on proving the privileged choice was unreasonable.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The sailor is privileged to remain during the storm but must compensate the owner for the actual damage to the pier.
choices.rationale: Private necessity prevents trespass liability for the entry while preserving strict responsibility for physical damage caused to the property.

explanation.testedIssue: The effect of private necessity on trespass liability and compensation for resulting damage.
explanation.governingRule: Private necessity privileges a reasonable intrusion on another's property to protect the actor or the actor's property from serious harm. The privilege is incomplete: the actor is generally liable for actual damage caused and the possessor may not forcibly eject the actor while the necessity continues.
explanation.application: The storm justified tying up despite the owner's objection, so the sailor is not an ordinary trespasser. Because the boat damaged the pier, he must pay the $8,000 loss.
explanation.commonTrap: Confusing private necessity with the complete privilege generally associated with public necessity.
explanation.memoryAid: Private necessity lets you stay, but you pay for the damage.

authorityNotes: Vincent v. Lake Erie Transportation Co.

questions.subject: Torts
questions.subtopic: Negligent entrustment as direct liability independent of agency
questions.stem: A car owner lent her car to her adult cousin for the weekend even though she knew that the cousin had repeatedly driven while intoxicated and had recently lost his license for reckless driving. The cousin was not the owner's employee or agent. While using the car for a purely personal trip, the cousin became intoxicated, sped through an intersection, and injured a cyclist. The cyclist sues the owner for negligent entrustment.
questions.call: Is the owner likely liable?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because knowingly lending to a dangerously incompetent driver can create direct liability.
choices.rationale: Negligent entrustment is based on the supplier's own negligence and does not require an employment or agency relationship.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because the cousin was not acting within the scope of employment for the owner.
choices.rationale: Scope of employment concerns vicarious liability; negligent entrustment is an independent direct-negligence theory.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because an owner is liable for another driver's conduct only when the owner is physically present in the car.
choices.rationale: Presence is not required when the owner's negligent decision to entrust the vehicle foreseeably enables the harm.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the cyclist proves that the car itself was defective.
choices.rationale: The dangerous condition was the known incompetence of the driver, not a defect in the vehicle.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether negligent entrustment permits recovery against a vehicle owner without an agency relationship.
explanation.governingRule: One who supplies a chattel for another's use may be directly liable for negligent entrustment when the supplier knows or should know that the user is likely, because of youth, inexperience, intoxication, or other incompetence, to use it in a manner involving unreasonable risk, and that risk causes harm.
explanation.application: The owner knew of repeated intoxicated and reckless driving but supplied the car anyway. The precise danger materialized, establishing breach and causation independent of respondeat superior.
explanation.commonTrap: Rejecting direct negligence merely because vicarious liability is unavailable.
explanation.memoryAid: Negligent entrustment is the owner's own negligence, not borrowed agency.

questions.subject: Torts
questions.subtopic: Commercial seller's strict liability to foreseeable bystander for manufacturing defect
questions.stem: A hardware retailer sold a new lawn mower manufactured by another company. Because of an assembly error at the factory, a blade-fastening bolt on that mower was materially weaker than every bolt called for by the design. The defect was not discoverable through the retailer's ordinary inspection. During the purchaser's first normal use, the bolt fractured and the blade flew across the purchaser's yard, injuring a neighbor walking on the sidewalk. The neighbor sues the retailer under strict products liability.
questions.call: Is the retailer likely liable?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because the neighbor neither purchased nor used the lawn mower.
choices.rationale: Privity and user status are generally unnecessary; strict products liability extends to foreseeable bystanders injured by a defective product.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because a commercial seller is strictly liable when a manufacturing defect foreseeably injures a bystander.
choices.rationale: Commercial sellers may be strictly liable for manufacturing defects existing when the product left their control, even without negligence and even to bystanders.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the retailer could not have discovered the defect through reasonable inspection.
choices.rationale: Strict products liability does not depend on the retailer's ability to detect the manufacturing defect or on proof of negligence.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the mower's manufacturer is insolvent or beyond the court's jurisdiction.
choices.rationale: A retailer's strict liability is not merely secondary to the manufacturer's collectability, although indemnity may be available between sellers.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a retailer is strictly liable to a bystander for a manufacturing defect it could not detect.
explanation.governingRule: A commercial supplier in the distribution chain is strictly liable for physical harm caused by a defective product that reaches the user or consumer without substantial change. A manufacturing defect exists when the product departs from its intended design, and foreseeable bystanders may recover without privity.
explanation.application: The weak bolt deviated from the intended design, existed before retail sale, and failed during normal first use. The retailer's nonnegligent inability to inspect is not a defense to the neighbor's strict-liability claim.
explanation.commonTrap: Importing negligence concepts or contractual privity into strict products liability.
explanation.memoryAid: Defect in the chain can make every commercial seller liable to foreseeable victims.

questions.subject: Torts
questions.subtopic: Defamation - private figure, public concern, negligence, and limits on presumed or punitive damages
questions.stem: A local newspaper negligently published a false article accusing a private accountant of embezzling money from a large community charity. The charity's finances had been the subject of extensive public debate. The newspaper had no knowledge of falsity and did not recklessly disregard the truth. The accountant proves that several clients terminated their engagements because of the article and proves the amount of the resulting financial loss. State law treats accusations of serious crime and professional dishonesty as defamation per se.
questions.call: What damages may the accountant constitutionally recover on these facts?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No damages, because a private figure must always prove actual malice when the publication concerns a matter of public interest.
choices.rationale: A private figure may recover compensation for proven actual injury upon a constitutionally sufficient showing such as negligence; actual malice is not always required.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Presumed and punitive damages solely because the statement is defamation per se under state law.
choices.rationale: When speech concerns a public matter, presumed or punitive damages generally require actual malice despite a state-law per se rule.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Compensatory damages for the proven actual injury, but not presumed or punitive damages absent proof of actual malice.
choices.rationale: A private plaintiff may recover proven actual damages based on negligence, while presumed and punitive damages for public-concern speech require knowing or reckless falsity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Punitive damages but not compensatory damages, because professional dishonesty is especially blameworthy.
choices.rationale: Punitive damages demand the higher actual-malice showing here, while proven compensatory loss is recoverable on negligence.

explanation.testedIssue: The constitutional damages rules for a private defamation plaintiff involving a matter of public concern.
explanation.governingRule: For defamatory speech on a matter of public concern, a private figure may recover proven actual injury upon a showing of fault at least amounting to negligence. The plaintiff may not recover presumed or punitive damages without proving actual malice—knowledge of falsity or reckless disregard—even if state law labels the statement defamation per se.
explanation.application: The accountant is private, the charity controversy is public, and the newspaper was negligent but lacked actual malice. The proven lost-client damages are compensable, but presumed and punitive awards are unavailable.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming defamation per se automatically permits presumed damages regardless of constitutional limits.
explanation.memoryAid: Private figure plus public concern: negligence buys actual damages; actual malice buys presumed or punitive.

authorityNotes: Dun & Bradstreet, Inc. v. Greenmoss Builders, Inc.
